compiled_state.title: As a practitioner, sprint detects codebase drift at entry and re-invokes implementation-plan
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
## Description

**What**: At sprint entry, before executing tasks, sprint compares the git commit history since task ticket creation against the file impact table from the original implementation plan. If drift is detected, implementation-plan is autonomously re-invoked.
**Why**: Codebase changes from other worktrees or sessions can invalidate the plan. Without a pre-flight check, the sprint executes against a stale decomposition and tasks fail for reasons that have nothing to do with the task descriptions.
**Scope**:
- IN: Sprint entry drift detection logic, git commit history comparison against file impact table, autonomous re-invocation of implementation-plan, REPLAN_TRIGGER: drift comment recording
- OUT: Transitive dependency checking (documented limitation — direct impact table files only), non-interactive mode handling

## Done Definitions
- When this story is complete, sprint entry checks git log since the task ticket creation timestamps against the implementation plan file impact table, and if any file in the table was modified by an external commit, implementation-plan is re-invoked
  ← Satisfies: "sprint compares git commit history since task ticket creation against the file impact table"
- When this story is complete, a REPLAN_TRIGGER: drift comment is recorded on the epic when drift is detected and re-planning is triggered
  ← Satisfies: "each trigger records a structured REPLAN_TRIGGER"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic

## Considerations
- [Testing] Needs behavioral tests with mock commit history and file impact tables
- [Reliability] Drift check covers direct impact table files only — transitive deps are a documented limitation

## Escalation Policy
**Escalation policy**: Escalate to the user whenever you do not have high confidence in your understanding of the work, approach, or intent. "High confidence" means clear evidence from the codebase or ticket context — not inference or reasonable assumption. When in doubt, stop and ask rather than guess.
